items.name: embedding init
items.note: Same distribution, but different library RNG order.
items.ref_src: Megatron model_provider instantiates nn.Embedding via ColumnParallelEmbedding; init via init_method=normal_(0, 0.006)
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py:GPT._init_weights — `nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=self.config.init_std)` on every nn.Embedding
items.impact: Same random seed (1337) produces different tensor values because module construction order differs (nano creates embedding → 9 blocks; Megatron creates embedding split by TP, per-layer init). Resulting embedding tensor is different bit-for-bit → training trajectory differs from step 1.

items.name: router init
items.note: User-confirmed: cybertron has a deepseek_v2 router with Kaiming init, but the 00196 job does NOT take that branch — it uses the Megatron init_method_std=0.006 path, identical to nano.
items.ref_src: Megatron ColumnParallelLinear (router weight) with init_method=normal_(0, 0.006)
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py MoERouter.linear re-initialized by GPT._init_weights apply() → normal_(0, 0.006)
items.impact: No divergence from init-distribution here.

items.name: expert weights init (routed)
items.nano: nano: stacked weights normal_(0, std=0.006) all 3 projs
items.note: Routed experts: gate_weight [144,512,160], up_weight [144,512,160], down_weight [144,160,512]
items.ref_src: Megatron MoE experts each individually initialized via scaled_init if enabled, else normal(0, 0.006)
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py MoEFFN.__init__ — single nn.init.normal_ call on stacked [E,C,H] tensor
items.impact: Same distribution and std but single-call vs per-expert-call consumes random numbers in different order. Expert[0] in nano might have init tensor != expert[0] in ref. Over 144 experts × 3 weights × 512*160 params each, cumulative random-state divergence is massive. Combined with router init diff, early routing decisions diverge markedly.

items.name: layer-norm weights init
items.note: Both initialize RMSNorm scale to 1; no divergence here

items.name: bias init
items.note: both: add_bias_linear=false, so no bias tensors exist — no divergence

items.name: optimizer name
items.note: ref: `adam` in Megatron (their "adam" = AdamW style with decoupled WD); nano: torch AdamW. Same math.
items.ref_src: Megatron optimizer = FusedAdam with decoupled weight_decay=0.1
items.nano_src: nanogpt configure_optimizers uses torch.optim.AdamW(fused=True)
items.impact: Numerically equivalent. Same update rule, same betas/eps. Any residual is from fused kernel impl details.

items.name: accumulate_allreduce_grads_in_fp32
items.note: ref: grad accumulation + DP all-reduce in fp32. nano: bf16 throughout (PyTorch DDP bucket reduces in param dtype = bf16).
items.ref_src: Megatron grad_accumulate_in_fp32=True stores gradient buffer as fp32
items.nano_src: nanogpt uses default torch DDP which reduces grads in param dtype (bf16)
items.impact: bf16 has only ~3 decimal digits of mantissa. Gradient averaging over 8 ranks in bf16 has additional rounding vs fp32. Per-step per-param error ~1e-4 relative; grows linearly in step count. Over 2000 steps the bias could explain 0.1–0.3 nat of the observed gap. Likely a real contributor.

items.name: use_distributed_optimizer
items.note: nano: no ZeRO-style sharded optimizer. Each rank holds full optimizer state.
items.ref_src: Megatron Distributed Optimizer: each rank holds 1/DP of optimizer state (exp_avg, exp_avg_sq)
items.nano_src: nanogpt: standard torch AdamW, full optimizer state on each rank
items.impact: Mathematically equivalent — distributed optimizer partitions the state across ranks but the per-parameter update is identical. No direct numerical divergence.

items.name: lr_warmup_init
items.note: nano warmup starts from 0 (implicit)

items.name: train_samples
items.note: nano train_samples implicitly = max_iters × global_bs

items.name: micro_batch_size
items.note: ref=4 samples per rank per micro-batch; nano=1. Multiple samples in one forward → LayerNorm/RMSNorm see bigger stats (though our RMSNorm is per-sample so this is moot).
items.ref_src: Megatron batch_size=4, forward passes [4,T,C] through TE fused layers
items.nano_src: nanogpt config batch_size=1, forward passes [1,T,C]
items.impact: Independent per-sample forward in nano ≠ batched forward in ref at the kernel level. bf16 matmul accumulation order differs. Per-step output drift ~1e-3 relative, grows with depth. Over 9 layers: cumulative ~1e-2 logit difference. Small but real.

items.name: expert_model_parallel_size
items.note: ref EP=4: each rank physically holds 36 of 144 experts; all-to-all dispatches tokens. nano EP=1: every rank holds ALL 144 experts, no cross-rank token shuffle.
items.ref_src: Megatron MoE layer — `moe_token_dispatcher_type=allgather` with EP=4 process group
items.nano_src: nanogpt MoEFFN — single-device expert bucket (torch.bmm with pad-to-max)
items.impact: Routing DECISIONS are identical (same sigmoid + top-K). What differs: gradient aggregation path. ref aggregates expert grads across its 2-DP ranks; nano across 8-DP ranks. Means nano has 4× the DP noise-reduction on expert weights. Expected effect: nano experts train more smoothly but diverge from ref due to different noise realization.

items.name: DP ranks (derived)
items.note: ref DP = world_size / TP / PP / EP = 8/4 = 2; nano DP = 8 (all ranks). Different gradient averaging partition.
items.ref_src: derived: world_size(8) / TP(1) / PP(1) / EP(4) = DP(2)
items.nano_src: derived: world_size(8) / TP(1) / PP(1) / EP(1) = DP(8)
items.impact: Gradient averaging: nano avg over 8 ranks, ref avg over 2 ranks (then EP shards). For any expert, ref update = avg of 2 samples gradients; nano update = avg of 8 samples gradients. nano has 4× lower gradient variance → smoother but possibly less greedy updates in early training. Systematically different convergence path.

items.name: DATA SAMPLE ROUTING PER RANK
items.ref: rank r reads samples r, r+2, r+4, ...  (DP=2, stride=2 in ref)
items.nano: rank r reads samples r, r+8, r+16, ...  (DP=8, stride=8 in nano)
items.note: Same total samples per step (64) but partitioned differently across ranks. Both nano and ref visit EVERY sample exactly once per "epoch"; just the order differs. nano get_batch_sequential initializes _seq_data_pos = ddp_rank * batch_size (line 189), advances by ddp_world_size * batch_size per call (line 209) — correct interleaved sharding.
items.ref_src: Megatron MCoreGPTDataset sampler: iterates by (dp_rank, dp_size) across blended index from BlendedDataset cache
items.nano_src: nanogpt/train.py line 189 (init _seq_data_pos[train] = ddp_rank * batch_size) and line 193-223 get_batch_sequential
items.impact: At each optim step, nano rank 0 sees samples {0,8,16,...,56} and ref rank 0 sees {0,2,4,...,14}. Different batches → different gradients even with identical init. Over 1000s of steps, trajectory systematically diverges from ref (observed: +1.8 nat at iter 1000). Cannot be eliminated without matching DP=2 + EP=4 partition (requires rewriting DDP setup).

items.name: eod_mask_loss
items.ref_src: cybertron/utils/mcore_utils.py:179 — `loss_mask[data == eod_token] = 0.0` where `data` = INPUT tokens (tokens[:-1])
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py GPT.forward — mask when TARGET==eod (one position later than ref)
items.impact: Off-by-one: ref masks loss at the position where input IS EOD; nano masks where target IS EOD. In practice near EOD boundaries only, small effect on loss magnitude (< 0.01 loss delta) but affects gradient routing through final FC.

items.name: accurate_attn_mask_eod_token
items.note: nano use_eod_attn_mask=True gates this on, otherwise it is None and behaves as plain causal.
items.ref_src: cybertron/utils/batchify_utils.py:63-88 — builds accurate_attn_mask tensor from EOD positions, passes to TE attention
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py GPT.forward — builds segment_id via cumsum, forms same-segment AND causal mask, passes to SDPA with is_causal=False
items.impact: Both produce mathematically the same mask, but different SDPA kernel paths: ref uses TE flash-attn with variable-length segments; nano uses SDPA dense bool mask. Dense mask can force SDPA off the flash-attn-2 fast path → slower and slightly different numerics (1e-3 relative). Observed: training slower when use_eod_attn_mask=True.

items.name: reset_position_ids
items.note: RoPE is position-based; nano always uses contiguous positions. Packed docs past EOD get "wrong" position — likely source of gap

items.name: SUSPECT: position ids reset on EOD
items.note: Both say no; but since reset_attention_mask=false with accurate_attn_mask=true, docs within a sequence share positions yet attn segments — behavior subtle
items.impact: RoPE encodes absolute position; if token at position 5000 is forced to only attend within doc segment [4800..5000] but still uses position=5000, attention scores systematically different from a fresh-segment view. Unclear if ref behaves the same.

items.name: router_scoring_func
items.note: nano uses torch.sigmoid in MoERouter.forward

items.name: routed_scaling_factor
items.note: nano hardcoded 1.0 (no scaling on topk weights post-norm)

items.name: moe_router_load_balancing_type
items.note: nano picks top-K per group (greedy)

items.name: seq_aux
items.note: nano MoEFFN computes per-sequence aux when alpha>0

items.name: seq_aux P_i normalization
items.nano: P_i^b = mean_t σ(l_i(t))                     [RAW sigmoid, NOT normalized]
items.note: ROOT CAUSE CANDIDATE for 1.8 nat gap
items.ref_src: cybertron_dots3.0_swa/cybertron/models/deepseek_v2/modules_deepseekv2.py:377 — `pii = pi.div(pi.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-20).mean(0)`
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py MoEFFN.forward (seq_aux block): `P = raw_scores.view(B,T,E).float().mean(dim=1)`
items.impact: Sigmoid outputs are independent per expert → sum ≈ E·sigmoid_mean ≈ E·0.5. Without normalization, my P is ~0.5; Megatron’s P is ~1/E = 1/144. My aux is ~72× larger. With α=0.0001, my effective aux magnitude ~7e-3 vs ref ~1e-4. This drives routing to balance too aggressively in early training, distorting everything downstream.

items.name: seq_aux f_i scale
items.ref: f_i^b = count * E / (T * K)   [inside the f term]
items.nano: f_i^b = count / T  (then multiply by E/K outside)
items.note: mathematically equivalent — just factor placement differs
items.ref_src: modules_deepseekv2.py:374 — `fii = fi.sum(0).div_(seq_len * K / E)`
items.nano_src: model.py MoEFFN.forward: `f = counts/T` then `aux = (E/K) * mean(Σ f*P)`

items.name: moe_aux_loss_coeff
items.note: disabled in both

items.name: moe_grouped_gemm
items.note: nano uses torch.bmm with pad-to-max-count; Megatron uses grouped_gemm (CUTLASS). Different kernel, same math.
items.ref_src: Megatron/TransformerEngine grouped_gemm CUTLASS kernel (per-expert variable-length batched matmul)
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py MoEFFN.forward — torch.bmm with every expert bucket padded to max(count) with zeros
items.impact: Padding adds ~10–30% extra compute on padding rows but keeps all experts aligned. Zero padding rows produce zero-output rows which are discarded via gather. Numerical result should match grouped_gemm to bf16 precision. Small residual drift ~1e-4 per bmm.

items.name: moe_token_dispatcher_type
items.note: nano: expert-sorted bmm; ref: allgather for EP>1

items.name: moe_permute_fusion
items.note: cosmetic; nano permute via argsort (no fusion)

items.name: moe_gating_fp32
items.note: nano computes router logits in fp32 (MoERouter line 270)

items.name: apply_rope_fusion
items.note: ref uses TE fused RoPE; nano uses manual complex-mult RoPE
items.ref_src: Megatron apply_rotary_pos_emb via TransformerEngine fused kernel
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py RotaryEmbedding — pre-compute cos/sin tables, apply rotate_half manually
items.impact: Both compute the same mathematical RoPE, but TE fused kernel uses fp32 intermediate for the rotation; nano does rotation in bf16. Max per-element rotation error ~1 ULP × n_head ≈ 1e-4. Over 9 layers, attention logits drift ~1e-3. Can affect soft-argmax routing probabilities on borderline experts.

items.name: RoPE position ids
items.note: both do not reset on EOD

items.name: attention impl
items.note: Different flash backends. Numerically similar but not identical
items.ref_src: NVTE_FLASH_ATTN=1 NVTE_FUSED_ATTN=1 → TransformerEngine flash-attn 2
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py CausalSelfAttention — torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention
items.impact: SDPA may dispatch to memory-efficient attention or flash-attn-2 depending on shape/contiguity. Numerical result differs at ~1e-3 relative. Over 9 layers + softmax amplification: attention output drift ~1e-2. Small but compounds with routing.

items.name: transformer_impl
items.note: ref uses NVIDIA TransformerEngine with fused kernels for attention+norm+linear; nano uses separate PyTorch modules.
items.ref_src: Megatron `transformer_impl=transformer_engine` — TE fused LayerNormLinear, attention, MLP
items.nano_src: nanogpt/model.py — separate RMSNorm, CausalSelfAttention, MLP/MoEFFN calls
items.impact: TE fused kernels do multiple ops in a single CUDA call with shared registers → different intermediate precision path. E.g., fused LayerNormLinear accumulates norm statistics in fp32 then applies linear in bf16; nano does norm in bf16 storage then bf16 linear. Cumulative drift across 9 layers: ~1e-3 logit relative error. Noticeable but small compared to other sources.

items.name: attention_softmax_in_fp32
items.note: ref always casts attention logits to fp32 before softmax. SDPA backend depends on flash-attn availability.
items.ref_src: TransformerEngine attention: `if attention_softmax_in_fp32: softmax(logits.float()).to(dtype)`
items.nano_src: nanogpt SDPA — internal behavior depends on kernel selected (flash-attn-2 uses fp32 accum; math backend may not)
items.impact: If SDPA picks flash-attn-2: equivalent to ref. If math backend: ~1e-3 softmax relative error per head per layer. Over 4 heads × 9 layers: compounding error ~1e-2. Mitigation: force `with sdp_kernel(enable_flash=True): ...`.

items.name: apply_query_key_layer_scaling
items.note: disabled both sides

items.name: cross_entropy_fusion_impl
items.note: both use eager F.cross_entropy

items.name: tokenizer_model path
items.note: nano uses same path when loaded (see tests/test_tokenizer_alignment)

items.name: blend dataset count
items.note: test_data_sampling_alignment asserts match

items.name: first 1024 (dataset_id, sample_id) sha256
items.note: from reports/data_sampling_alignment.json

items.name: train samples consumed
items.note: 2000-step short run consumed 2000*64=128000 samples, 27% of full

items.name: val dataset
items.note: prepare_cybertron_data.py VAL_DATA_PATH

items.name: deterministic mode
items.note: ref bitwise_aligned=false → fastest TE kernels (may be non-deterministic). nano deterministic=true → slower deterministic kernels only.
items.ref_src: Megatron args.bitwise_aligned controls TransformerEngine determinism flags
items.nano_src: nanogpt/train.py — torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True), cudnn.deterministic=True
items.impact: Different kernels chosen → different numerical rounding. Example: non-deterministic reduction uses atomicAdd; deterministic uses stable tree-reduce — result off by ~ULP per accumulated element. Over attention with 8192 positions, residual errors ~1e-3 per layer × 9 layers = ~1e-2 output drift.

items.name: seed
items.note: Megatron default seed is 1234 (overridable), ours nano 1337.
items.ref_src: Megatron args.seed (default 1234 unless yaml sets `seed`)
items.nano_src: nanogpt config/cybertron_moe_196.py: seed = 1337
items.impact: Entirely different random stream → entirely different init tensors → entirely different training trajectory from step 0. This alone could explain a significant fraction of the 1.8 nat offset.

items.name: dataloader_type
items.note: nano get_batch_sequential; ref `single`. Semantically equivalent for our case.
items.ref_src: Megatron `single` mode: iterate the blended sampler once, one sample per batch element
items.nano_src: nanogpt/train.py:get_batch_sequential — read block_size+1 tokens from mmap, stride by ddp_world_size*batch_size
items.impact: No direct numerical impact — both produce the same sequence of samples when fed the same tokenized cache.
